<!-- GENERATED by scripts/sync_agent_assets.py from agents/skills/foam/references/forces.md — edit the source and re-run the script; do not edit this copy. -->

# Force coefficients (on top of parse_force_coefficients)

`parse_force_coefficients(case_dir)` turns a case's forceCoeffs output into
typed coefficient facts — Cd/Cl/Cm (plus the Cl(f)/Cl(r) fore/aft lift
split) with first/final values and tail-window statistics, and the header's
reference values (magUInf, lRef, Aref, CofR, direction vectors) so a wrong
normalization is visible before a number is trusted. Numbers are computed
from the file, never averaged by eye, at the same token cost whether the
run wrote a hundred samples or a million. When the case has a run-ledger
row the tool also stamps a compact summary into its Key result cell —
`Cd=<tail mean> Cl=<tail mean> (tail mean)` — so run history carries the
number that mattered. No forceCoeffs output or a headerless/empty dat file
is a typed error pointing back at this recipe; an ambiguous choice between
several force function objects is a typed error naming the candidates —
never fabricated statistics.

Discovery: the tool reads
`postProcessing/<function>/<newest time>/forceCoeffs.dat` — the newest time
directory wins (restart layouts). Several force function objects with
output and no explicit `function_name` is a typed error naming the
candidates; pass `function_name` to choose.

**Reach for the tool first.** Only when it reports no forceCoeffs output do
you need the recipe below — and then you follow it, not the folklore.

## The recipe: how forces are actually computed on Foundation v10

**The reliable universal path is a `forceCoeffs` function object active
during a solver run.** Cost is near zero on top of the solve. Everything
else is a fallback with solver-family caveats — all of the below was
verified live on v10, not assumed:

- **icoFoam family: re-run the solver with the object added.** `icoFoam`
  has NO `-postProcess` option (FATAL: `Invalid option`). Adding the
  function object to `controlDict` and re-running IS the post-hoc path —
  cheap for the short cases this family runs, and `run_case` reruns start
  from scratch anyway.
- **simpleFoam / pimpleFoam families: `<solver> -postProcess` exists.**
  With the function object in `controlDict`, it replays the function
  objects over the saved time directories without re-solving. The series is
  only as fine as the write interval — coefficients exist only where fields
  were written. Unsure whether a solver supports it? `<solver> -help` lists
  its options.
- **Standalone `postProcess` CANNOT compute viscous forces — do not walk
  this path.** Without `-fields` it dies with `Could not find U, p`; even
  with `-fields "(U p)"` and a laminar `constant/momentumTransport` file
  present it dies with `No valid model for viscous stress calculation`.
  The WHY: the utility loads fields from disk but has no solver, so no
  momentum-transport model is constructed in its registry — the viscous
  stress tensor cannot be formed, and pressure-only forces would be wrong,
  so it refuses. This is the three-FATAL-errors folklore path
  (`icoFoam -postProcess` → `postProcess -func ...` → `postProcess -fields
  ...`); the function-object-at-run-time recipe avoids all three.

So: force question known up front → put the object in `controlDict` before
the run (the sanctioned exception to the function-object ban — see
[file-generation.md](file-generation.md)). Force question after the fact →
`-postProcess` where the solver has it, otherwise re-run with the object.

## A worked minimal block (harvested from the cavity, correct v10 shape)

In `system/controlDict`:

```
functions
{
    forceCoeffs1
    {
        type        forceCoeffs;
        libs        ("libforces.so");
        patches     (movingWall);       // the patches the force acts on
        rho         rhoInf;             // incompressible: fields carry p/rho
        rhoInf      1;                  // the density that makes forces dimensional
        magUInf     1;                  // MUST match the true free stream (here: lid speed)
        lRef        0.1;                // moment reference length (cavity side)
        Aref        1e-3;               // 2D: reference length x depth (lid 0.1 x 0.01)
        CofR        (0.05 0.05 0.005);  // moments are taken about this point
        liftDir     (0 1 0);
        dragDir     (1 0 0);
        pitchAxis   (0 0 1);
    }
}
```

With no write controls specified it samples every time step (the cavity
harvest: 101 samples over 0–0.5 s at deltaT 0.005). The output lands in
`postProcessing/forceCoeffs1/<startTime>/forceCoeffs.dat`.

## Normalization pitfalls (wrong reference values = wrong numbers)

- **Aref on 2D cases**: the projected reference AREA — reference length
  times the mesh's depth (the `empty` direction), not the length alone.
  The cavity harvest used the lid area: 0.1 x 0.01 = 1e-3. Passing the
  length alone scales every coefficient by 1/depth (here: a factor-100
  error).
- **lRef vs chord**: lRef only normalizes the moment coefficient Cm (for
  airfoils: the chord). Cd/Cl do not use it — a wrong lRef corrupts Cm
  while Cd/Cl look fine.
- **CofR**: moments are computed about this point. A CofR at the wrong
  place (leading edge vs quarter-chord vs body centre) changes Cm's value
  and even its sign — state which point a reported Cm is about.
- **magUInf must match the true free stream** (the inlet/lid boundary
  value). Coefficients scale with 1/magUInf² — a factor-2 velocity error
  is a factor-4 coefficient error.
- **Incompressible solvers and rhoInf**: icoFoam/simpleFoam-family fields
  carry kinematic pressure (p/rho), so use `rho rhoInf;` plus a `rhoInf`
  value. The COEFFICIENTS normalize consistently for any rhoInf (it enters
  force and normalization alike), but DIMENSIONAL forces and moments scale
  with it — quote forces in newtons only with the real density set.

## Reading the tool's output

- **The tail window**: statistics are over the last 20% of samples
  (rounded up, floored at 10, capped at all when fewer exist), and the
  window actually used — sample count, start/end time — is in the output.
  Quote coefficient answers as the tail mean over that stated window.
- **When the tail mean is trustworthy**: the tail must be past the
  transient. Settled looks like `tail_min`/`tail_max` tight around
  `tail_mean` and `final` ≈ `tail_mean` (the cavity harvest: min = mean =
  max to 6 figures). Still-transient looks like a wide tail spread or
  `final` drifting away from the mean — extend `endTime` and rerun before
  quoting a number. Deliberately unsteady flows (vortex shedding): the
  tail mean is a mean over the oscillation and `tail_min`/`tail_max` give
  the amplitude envelope — say so instead of presenting one settled value.
- **Steady solvers**: samples are iterations, not physical time — the tail
  is meaningful only if the run converged; cross-check `parse_solver_log`
  (and [convergence.md](convergence.md)) before trusting it.
- **Check the echoed reference block** against what you intended: the
  header's magUInf/lRef/Aref/CofR are what the numbers were normalized
  with, so a wrong Aref is visible right there.
- **Signs follow the direction vectors**: Cd is the fluid force on the
  patches resolved along `dragDir`, Cl along `liftDir`. A negative Cd means
  the force points against `dragDir` — the cavity's moving lid reads
  Cd = -2.501 because the fluid it drives pulls back against the lid's
  motion. Check the vectors before declaring drag negative.
- **The fore/aft split**: Cl(f) + Cl(r) = Cl and Cl(f) - Cl(r) = 2·Cm —
  the moment balance for external-aero questions.
- **The Key result cell**: `Cd=<tail mean:.4g> Cl=<tail mean:.4g> (tail
  mean)` — e.g. `Cd=-2.501 Cl=0.1275 (tail mean)` — stamped into the
  case's ledger row as a server-owned side effect (machine-owned cell, not
  part of `set_run_note`'s surface). A case without a row still returns
  the full analysis, unstamped; re-parsing re-stamps idempotently.
